title: Who Is Really in Charge?
sub_domain_name: Informal Power, Corporate Influence & Parallel Governance

required.title: [Read Aloud] Who's in Charge?
required.author: sections from the student packet below

required.title: [Look Together] Advertisements from magazines, television, or websites

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Click, Clack, Moo: Cows That Type
suggested.author: Doreen Cronin

suggested.title: [Activity] A trip to the grocery store
[Look Together] The evening news or a news website

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Manufacturing Consent
supplementary.author: Edward S. Herman and Noam Chomsky

competence_markers.description: The student can identify at least three forms of power that exist outside government and explain how they affect people's lives
The student can distinguish between advertising and information and explain the difference using real examples
The student can explain in simple terms how companies try to influence the rules that governments make
The student demonstrates the habit of asking "who paid for this?" or "who benefits?" when encountering ads, news, or claims
The student can compare two news reports of the same event and identify differences in framing or emphasis
The student understands that the arrangement of stores, websites, and media is designed to influence their choices
The student can describe how money creates access to politicians and why this matters
The student engages with these topics with curiosity and observation, not with fear or cynicism — the goal is awareness, not paranoia

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a kind of power that does not come from the government.
narration_prompts.detail: Where does it come from? How does it affect people?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between an advertisement and information?
narration_prompts.detail: How can you tell which one you are looking at?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the grocery store visit.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice about how the store was set up? Who arranged it that way, and why?

narration_prompts.prompt: When you see a news story, who decided to tell you that story?
narration_prompts.detail: Why might they have chosen it? What might you not be hearing about?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does "who benefits?" mean, and why is it a useful question?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example from your own life where you used this question.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a company or organization that is not the government but has power over people's lives.
narration_prompts.detail: What power do they have? Who gave them that power?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is it important to notice who paid for something — an ad, a news channel, a rule?
narration_prompts.detail: What does knowing that help you understand?

cross_connections.description: The formal government machinery studied in 1.4.5 is what informal power influences. The student who understands bureaucracy can now see the external forces acting on it — lobbying, campaign donations, the revolving door between government and industry.

The forms of government studied in 1.4.2 all face the same challenge: informal power that operates alongside and sometimes overrides the formal structure. Democracy is supposed to be ruled by voters, but voters are influenced by the media and advertising systems studied here.

The ethical question of fairness — explored in 1.4.4 — becomes concrete when the student sees that some people have more influence over the rules than others. Is that fair? When is it acceptable and when is it not?

Understanding that money creates influence is both a civic insight and a financial one. The student who understands how wealth translates into power has additional motivation for the financial literacy developed in 4.1.

The narrative management concepts introduced here — advertising, news selection, framing — are the entry point for the deeper ideology immunity work in 1.7. The student who can spot an ad is building the same muscle that will later spot propaganda.

Narrating observations about power and influence builds the same expression skills developed in 3.2.1. The student who can articulate what they noticed about the grocery store layout is practicing organized communication.

content.why_this_matters:
You have already learned that the government has elected leaders — like the president or the mayor — and hired workers who do the daily work of running things. You know about laws, courts, and rules. But here is something that most civics classes never tell you: **the government is not the only place where power lives.**

Think about it. A company that makes the food most people eat has enormous influence over what people put in their bodies. A company that runs the television channels or the websites most people look at decides what stories people hear. A very wealthy person who donates money to politicians can get meetings and conversations that an ordinary person cannot. None of these people were elected. None of them took an oath of office. But they have real power over real people's lives.

This packet is about learning to see power where it is not wearing a name tag. Not all power comes from government. Not all rules come from laws. And the people who have the most influence over your daily life are not always the ones you can vote for — or vote against. Learning to notice this is one of the most important skills you will ever develop.

This is not about being suspicious of everyone. It is about being observant. You are learning to ask a simple question that most people never learn to ask: **"Who benefits from this?"**
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The awareness that power exists outside government** — the student begins to notice that companies, wealthy individuals, and organizations can influence rules and decisions without being elected
2. **The habit of asking "who paid for this?"** — advertising, sponsorships, and messaging as forms of influence the student encounters daily
3. **The distinction between advertising and information** — learning to tell the difference between someone trying to help you understand and someone trying to get you to buy, believe, or do something
4. **Basic understanding of how big companies influence rules** — the concept that businesses try to shape the rules that affect them
5. **The concept that news and stories are chosen by someone** — not everything important gets reported, and what gets reported reflects someone's choices
6. **The foundation for healthy skepticism** — not paranoia or distrust of everything, but the habit of looking at who is behind a message before accepting it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Power Without a Title

You have learned about presidents, governors, and mayors. You have learned about the workers who run government agencies. All of these people have titles. They have offices. You can look them up and find out who they are.

But there are other people who have enormous power and no government title at all.

Think about the company that makes the phone or tablet your family uses. That company decided what you can and cannot do with that device. They decided what apps are available. They decided what information to collect about you. They did not pass a law. They did not win an election. They just built something that millions of people depend on, and then they made the rules for how it works.

Or think about a very large store — a store so big that it is the main place people in a whole town buy their food and supplies. If that store decides to stop selling something, it becomes very hard for people in that town to get it. That store did not make a law. But it made a decision that changed what people can buy.

Power is not only about laws and elections. Power is about who can make decisions that affect other people's lives — whether those people agreed to it or not.

### Part 2: Why Companies Care About Rules

Imagine you and your friends want to set up lemonade stands on your street. You are all competing to sell the most lemonade. Then one friend's parent goes to the town council and suggests a new rule: "All lemonade must be made with organic lemons certified by an approved inspector." It sounds like a safety rule. But your friend's family happens to own the only organic lemon farm in town.

Now everyone else has to buy expensive organic lemons from your friend's family — or shut down their stand. A rule that sounds like it is about safety is actually about giving one person an advantage over everyone else.

This happens in the real world. Companies spend enormous amounts of money trying to influence the rules that governments make. Sometimes they want rules that help them. Sometimes they want rules that hurt their competitors. Sometimes they want rules to go away entirely. The money they spend on this — talking to politicians, hiring people to argue their case, donating to campaigns — is called **lobbying**.

Lobbying is legal. It is not automatically bad. Sometimes companies have good information about how a rule would work, and sharing that information helps the government make better decisions. But it means that the rules you live under were not made only by the people you voted for. They were shaped, in part, by the companies and organizations that had the money and motivation to influence those people.

### Part 3: Advertising — When Someone Wants Your Attention

You see advertisements every day. On television, on the computer, on billboards, on the sides of buses, on the packaging of the food you eat. Ads are so common that you might not even notice them anymore. That is exactly what makes them powerful.

An advertisement is not information. It is persuasion. The person who made the ad does not want to help you understand the world. They want you to do something — buy a product, go to a place, believe a certain thing. There is nothing wrong with this — it is honest when the ad is clearly an ad. But the student who learns to notice "this is an ad" — even when it does not look like an ad — has an enormous advantage over the student who does not.

Here is the key question to practice: **"Does this person want to help me, or do they want something from me?"**

Sometimes the answer is both. Sometimes a helpful person also benefits from helping you. But the question is always worth asking.

### Part 4: Who Chooses the News?

When you watch the news or read a story on a website, someone chose to tell you that story. They chose it out of hundreds — maybe thousands — of things that happened that day. Why that story and not a different one?

News organizations are run by people. Those people have bosses. Those bosses have owners. The owners have interests — sometimes business interests, sometimes political interests, sometimes both. This does not mean every news story is a lie. Most journalists try hard to be accurate and fair. But it does mean that the selection of which stories to tell, how much time to give them, and how to frame them is influenced by the people who own and run the news organization.

Here is a useful thought experiment: **"What happened today that I am not being told about?"** You will not always know the answer. But the habit of asking the question protects you from assuming that whatever you are shown is the complete picture.

### Part 5: The Grocery Store Lesson

Walk into any grocery store and look at the cereal aisle. Notice where the sugary cereals are placed — at the eye level of children. That is not an accident. The cereal company paid the store to put their product exactly there, because they know children will see it and ask for it.

This is a tiny example of something much bigger: companies arrange the world around you to influence your choices. The layout of a store, the design of a website, the order of search results on the internet — all of these are shaped by someone's interests. The question is always: whose interests?

You are not powerless in this. Once you see it, you cannot un-see it. The student who notices that the cereal is placed at their eye level on purpose is already harder to manipulate than the student who does not.

### Part 6: Rich People and Influence

This is a simple observation, not a complaint: people with more money have more influence over the rules than people with less money.

A person who can donate a large amount of money to a politician's campaign gets to meet that politician. They get to explain what they care about. They get to ask for things. A person who donates nothing — or cannot afford to donate — does not get that meeting.

This does not mean every politician is corrupt or every rich person is trying to cheat. It means that the system is built in a way where money creates access. Access creates influence. And influence shapes the rules everyone lives under.

The student who understands this is not angry or cynical. They are informed. They know that the rules they live under were shaped by a process in which some voices were louder than others — and that the loudest voices are not always the wisest ones.

### Part 7: Organizations That Are Not the Government But Act Like It

There are organizations in the world that are not governments and not companies, but they still have real power over people's lives. These are sometimes called **non-governmental organizations** — NGOs for short.

Some do wonderful work: feeding hungry people, helping after disasters, protecting animals, building schools. Others are more complicated. An organization that decides which problems are "important" and which are not has the power to direct attention and money toward some things and away from others. An organization that sets standards — for food, for education, for health — has the power to define what counts as acceptable, even though nobody voted for them.

The key lesson is not that these organizations are bad. Many of them do real good. The lesson is that power does not only live in government buildings. It lives in any organization that can make decisions affecting other people — and the student who notices this is better prepared than the student who does not.

### Part 8: The Most Important Question You Can Learn to Ask

Here it is, the question that ties everything in this packet together:

**"Who benefits from this?"**

When you see a rule, ask: who benefits from this rule? When you see an ad, ask: who benefits if I believe this? When you hear a news story, ask: who benefits from me hearing this story right now? When someone tells you something is dangerous or safe, important or unimportant, ask: who benefits from me believing that?

This is not the same as being suspicious of everyone. Most people are honest most of the time. Most rules exist for good reasons. Most news stories are basically accurate. But the habit of asking "who benefits?" is like a flashlight — it helps you see things that are real but not obvious. And once you learn to carry this flashlight, you will be amazed at how much you start to notice.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing]
Read the core content aloud, one or two parts per session. After each part, the student narrates what they heard and connects it to their own experience. The narration is the assessment — listen for whether the student is beginning to notice influence in their daily life.

### Study 2: Ad Detective [Early in the Packet]
Collect five advertisements — from magazines, websites, TV, or packaging. For each one, the student identifies: What does this want me to do? Who paid for this? What feelings is it trying to create? Is there anything it is not telling me? Make this a game, not a lecture. The student becomes an "Ad Detective."

### Study 3: The Grocery Store Walk [Mid-Packet]
Visit a grocery store together with the specific purpose of noticing how the store is designed to influence choices. Where are certain products placed? What is at the checkout counter? What is at a child's eye level? The student narrates their observations afterward.

### Study 4: Who Owns This? [Mid-Packet]
Pick three things the student encounters regularly — a TV channel, a website, a favorite product. Together, look up who owns each one. Is it a small company or part of a giant company? Does the same company own other things? The student narrates what they discovered and what surprised them.

### Study 5: News Comparison [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Find the same news story reported by two different sources. Read or watch both versions together. What is the same? What is different? Why might the differences exist? The student is not being asked to determine which version is "right" — they are being asked to notice that the same event can be presented differently.

### Study 6: The "Who Benefits?" Game [Ongoing from Mid-Packet]
Make "who benefits?" a regular practice. When the student encounters a new rule, a commercial, a news story, or a claim — pause and ask together: who benefits from this? This becomes a family habit, not just a school exercise.

### Study 7: Drawing the Invisible Web [End of Packet]
The student draws a picture showing how different kinds of power connect. In the middle, put a family. Around them, draw the government, companies, news organizations, schools, and other organizations that influence the family's life. Draw lines showing the connections. Which ones did the family choose? Which ones were they born into? This makes visible the web of influence that most people never see.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Noticing Informal Power
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously identifies examples of non-governmental power in their daily life. They notice that companies, media, and wealthy individuals make decisions that affect everyone. They can give specific examples without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish between governmental and non-governmental power. Everything powerful is just "the government" or "the boss." No specific examples emerge.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you providing enough concrete examples in daily life? Are you doing the grocery store visit, the ad collection, and the news comparison? These activities are not optional — they are the primary teaching mechanism.

### 2. Ad and Influence Literacy
**Developing well:** The student can look at an advertisement and identify what it wants them to do, what feelings it is trying to create, and who paid for it. They begin to notice persuasion in unexpected places — product placement, packaging design, website layout.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish between information and persuasion. They take advertisements at face value. They do not notice when something is designed to influence them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the Ad Detective activity? Have you made it a game? Children who enjoy the detective frame engage much more deeply than children who feel lectured at.

### 3. "Who Benefits?" as a Habit
**Developing well:** The student begins asking "who benefits?" unprompted — in response to commercials, rules, news stories, or claims from peers. The question has become a natural part of how they process information.

**Developing slowly:** The student only asks the question when directly prompted. It has not become a habit.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling the question yourself in daily life? The most powerful teaching mechanism is for the student to hear you ask "who benefits from this?" in natural conversation — not just during packet time.

### 4. Calibration — Curiosity Without Cynicism
**Developing well:** The student is observant and curious about influence without being frightened, angry, or distrustful of everything. They can acknowledge that most people are honest most of the time while also noticing when someone has an interest in persuading them.

**Developing slowly:** The student either dismisses the material ("everything is fine, nobody is trying to trick me") or becomes anxious and suspicious ("everyone is lying, nothing is real"). Both responses indicate the material is not landing correctly.

**Diagnostic questions:** If the student is dismissive, they may need more concrete examples that make the invisible visible. If the student is anxious, you are probably delivering the material with too much alarm. Dial back the intensity. Emphasize the game-like quality of observation. Reassure them that noticing influence does not mean the world is dangerous — it means they are getting smarter.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making It About Your Politics
The single most common failure mode for this packet. The mentor has strong views about media bias, corporate corruption, or political influence — and uses the packet as a vehicle for those views. The student absorbs the mentor's conclusions instead of developing their own observational capacity. If you catch yourself saying "and that's why you can't trust Company X" or "this is why Party Y is corrupt," you have left the pedagogical lane.

### Scaring the Student
Young children can be frightened by the idea that powerful forces are shaping their world invisibly. The material must be presented as observation, not revelation of threat. The tone should be: "Isn't it interesting that..." not "You should be worried that..."

### Skipping the Concrete Activities
The grocery store visit, the ad collection, the news comparison, the "who owns this?" research — these are not busywork. They are the primary learning mechanism. The student who only hears about influence in the abstract learns much less than the student who sees it with their own eyes in a cereal aisle.

### Going Too Deep Too Soon
At Stretching Branches, the student does not need to understand regulatory capture, the revolving door, or managed democracy. They need to notice that the cereal is at their eye level on purpose. Stay at the concrete level. The systemic analysis comes at Weathering Storms and C.

### Creating a Conspiracy Theorist
There is a real danger of teaching a child that "nothing is what it seems" and producing a person who distrusts everything. The antidote is calibration: most things are roughly what they seem. Most people are basically honest. Most news stories are basically accurate. But the habit of checking — of asking one more question before accepting a claim — is a skill, not a pathology. If the student starts seeing conspiracies everywhere, pull back and re-emphasize the calibration.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Power Outside Government
**Strong:** Names specific examples — a company, a wealthy person, a media organization — and explains how their power works without government authority. May connect to their own experience.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between governmental and non-governmental power. "The government does everything."

### Prompt 2: Advertising Versus Information
**Strong:** Clearly explains the difference with examples. Can identify specific techniques used in ads they have seen. May express some delight in spotting manipulation.
**Weak:** "Ads tell you about stuff." No distinction between persuasion and information.

### Prompt 3: Grocery Store Observations
**Strong:** Describes specific observations — product placement, packaging design, the layout of aisles — and connects them to the concept of deliberate influence. Personal reactions are engaged and curious.
**Weak:** "The store was normal." No observations about design or placement.

### Prompt 4: News Selection
**Strong:** Can articulate that someone chooses which stories to tell and that this choice matters. May wonder about what they are not being told. Shows awareness that the same event can be presented differently.
**Weak:** "The news tells you what happened." No awareness of selection or framing.

### Prompt 5: "Who Benefits?"
**Strong:** Explains the question clearly and gives a real example from their own experience. The example shows genuine understanding, not just parroting.
**Weak:** Can repeat the question but cannot apply it. No real example.

### Prompt 6: Non-Governmental Organizations
**Strong:** Names a specific organization or company and explains what power it has over people's lives. Can articulate that this power was not given by voters.
**Weak:** Cannot name any non-governmental power center or explain how it works.
